petitioner_background: The Petitioner is a singer from Venezuela who has performed with various ensembles and Latin musicians in her native country and in the United States. She sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)). The Director of the Nebraska Service Center denied the petition, finding she met only one of the ten initial evidentiary criteria (judging), and she appealed that decision.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted a copy of her award certificate reflecting receipt of the Mara de Oro (Golden Mara) award from the Fundacion Mara de Oro de Venezuela e Internacional, designating her as '[category] of the Year.' She also submitted a document dated November 2016 bearing the logo of the Fundacion Mara de Oro de Venezuela e Internacional, describing the award as a 'prestigious award' whose mission is 'to enhance the work of revealing personalities of radio, television, cinema, theater, dance, advertising, culture, politics and sports,' and stating that 'candidates are elected at the national level and from all over the world, without exception.' The document listed names of other 'important personalities' and 'prestigious journalistic institutions' who previously won the award. Additionally, she submitted an undated letter from the Fundacion Mara de Oro de Venezuela e Internacional addressed to her, providing general information about the foundation, asserting the award is 'the only award that has certification in the country,' and stating it is given based upon 'the verdict of the [] people, according to the results of surveys conducted in different media ... and the consensus of the Board of Directors of this Foundation.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that while the Petitioner documented her receipt of the Mara de Oro award, the record did not establish that the award is a nationally or internationally recognized award for excellence in her field. The November 2016 document was not a letter and was not attributed to or signed by a responsible authority for the organization, so the AAO could not determine the actual source of the information, limiting its probative value. The AAO noted that although the Petitioner argued 'the location of the bestowment of the award does not lessen its importance or determine that it is a local or regional prize,' she failed to submit evidence of national or international recognition such as national or widespread local coverage of her award in arts, entertainment, or general media showing that Mara de Oro winners receive media coverage commensurate with a nationally or internationally recognized award in the entertainment industry. The record also lacked pertinent background information about the award from its organizer or another reliable source, such as a complete list of award categories, the various levels of awards given, the criteria used to grant awards, or whether all awards are deemed national in scope. The AAO cited relevant considerations including the criteria used to grant the award, its national or international significance, the number of awardees, and any limitations on competitors.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence that the Petitioner participated as a member of the jury for the Festival Internacional Orquidea de Oro.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the Petitioner fulfilled the judging criterion under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv) based on her participation as a member of the jury for the Festival Internacional Orquidea de Oro.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner submitted evidence and argument on appeal for the membership in associations criterion, though the specific evidence is not detailed in the decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO declined to reach this criterion, stating: 'While she argues and submits evidence for one additional criterion on appeal, relating to membership in associations, we need not reach this additional ground.' Because the Petitioner could not fulfill the initial evidentiary requirement of three criteria regardless of the outcome on membership, the AAO reserved the issue, citing INS v. Bagamasbad, 429 U.S. 24, 25-26 (1976).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The Petitioner initially submitted evidence related to the published material criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) before the Director.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that the Petitioner did not satisfy this criterion. The Petitioner did not contest this issue on appeal, so the AAO deemed it waived, citing Matter of M-A-S-, 24 I&N Dec. 762, 767 n.2 (BIA 2009).

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the Petitioner satisfied only the judging criterion and thus failed to meet the initial evidentiary requirement of at least three of the ten criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Because the Petitioner could not meet this threshold, the AAO reserved the membership criterion issue and found no need to conduct the final merits determination referenced in Kazarian. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the Petitioner established the acclaim and recognition required for the classification sought. The AAO emphasized that the classification is highly restrictive and intended for individuals already at the top of their fields rather than progressing toward the top, and found that the significance of the Petitioner's work was not indicative of sustained national or international acclaim or consistent with a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' as contemplated by Congress. The record did not contain sufficient evidence establishing that she is among the upper echelon in her field.
legal_citations: Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)

USCIS Policy Memorandum PM 602-0005.1, Evaluation of Evidence Submitted with Certain Form I-140 Petitions; Revisions to the Adjudicator's Field Manual (AFM) Chapter 22.2, AFM Update ADJ 1-14 (Dec. 22, 2010)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO noted a procedural waiver issue: the Petitioner did not contest the Director's denial under the published material criterion on appeal, so the AAO deemed that issue waived under Matter of M-A-S-. Additionally, the AAO reserved ruling on the membership in associations criterion under INS v. Bagamasbad because it was unnecessary to the disposition. No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
